**To My Dearest Daughter,**

My child, my only joy, my heart’s last flicker—  
You’ve not heard my voice in five years,  
And now, the night is black, the sea is wild,  
The tempest howls at the cliff, and I’m not sure  
If I’ll see dawn.  

But I write this, in case I don’t.  
In case I’m not here to greet you,  
In case the storm takes me, or the sea’s wrath  
Swallows my bones, or the wind’s cry drowns me—  
You’ll know I loved you, and I’ve always been  
Your shield, your light, your last tether.  

You’ve not come to me, not once,  
But I’ve never stopped thinking of you,  
Not once, not once, not once.  
I’ve kept the lantern lit, not for the world,  
But for you, for your safe harbor,  
For your dreams, for your tomorrow.  

If I’m not here, if the storm wins,  
Then know this: I’ve loved you,  
I’ve held the light, I’ve stood alone,  
I’ve waited for your return,  
And I’ve not regretted a single day.  

So take this, and take heart.  
Take courage, take the lantern’s flame.  
And if you’re out there, in the dark,  
Know this: I’ve always been

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not, for the storm that now rages upon these shores is of such fury that I scarce know if I shall see dawn. Yet, as the keeper of this lighthouse, I must write, for it is my duty, and perhaps, in this final hour, it may be my final act of love.

  

 I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know not if you would have written, had you the chance. But I must confess, I have thought of you oft, and the years have been long, and the silence between us has been a wound that I have not dared to mend. I have been a fool, I know, to let you know that I have not been the same man, nor the same father, but I have been a wretched man, and I have been the same father, in a way that I have not known how to say. 

 I have not written because I feared that my words would be too much, or too little, or too late. But now, as the storm gathers, I fear that I may not have the chance to write again. I have been a fool, I know, to let you know that I have not been the same man, nor the same father, but I have been a wretched man, and I have been the same father, in a way that I have not known how to

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds thee in good health, though I fear the winds of time have blown too harshly between us. It hath been five long years since our last correspondence, and I confess, my heart hath been heavy with the silence that hath passed between us. Yet now, as I write these words, the storm doth gather upon the horizon, and the sea doth roar with a fury that doth not bide. I do not know whether I shall see another dawn, and thus I write with a hand that doth shake, and a heart that doth weep.

Thou art my only child, and though the world hath been cruel to me, I do not seek to make thee sorrow. Yet I doth think of thee oft, and in my dreams, thy face doth haunt me, as if thou wert still by my side, and we might yet speak of our old days. I doth remember the time when thou wert but a little maid, and I didst teach thee the stars, and thou didst look up to me with eyes that shone like the light of the moon. And though the years have passed, I doth still see thee in my heart, as if thou wert still my daughter, and I were still thy father.

Now, as the storm doth gather, and the waves doth rise, I doth think that it is not for me to hold back

**To My Dearest Daughter,**

My darling, my dearest child—  
You must not be here, not in this cursed lighthouse,  
Not in this wretched place where the sea roars and the wind howls.  
But I write this letter with trembling hands and a heart like a storm,  
For you, for you, for the day I might not see again.  

Five years have passed since you left, since you walked out of my life  
Like a ghost, like a shadow, like a dream I cannot hold.  
I’ve been here, alone, with the beacon’s light and the sea’s wrath,  
And the silence between us has grown like a tide—unstoppable, unkind.  

But tonight, my daughter, the storm comes—  
A beast of wind and water, a tempest that will tear the sky.  
I’ve not seen the sky in five years, not since you left my side.  
And I do not know if I’ll live to see the dawn, or if I’ll meet the night.  

But I write this letter, for you, with a hope like a flame,  
That you’ll find me here, if I’m still here, if the sea has not won.  
For I’ve not given up, not yet, not in the grave of the waves.  
And I’ve not forgotten you, not in the silence, not in the dark.  

So, my child,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and spirits than I have been of late. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I confess, my heart aches with each passing day. I have often found myself in the quiet of this lighthouse, staring out at the sea, and wondering what path you have taken in life, and whether you think of me at all.

The nights have been long, and the solitude of this place has grown heavier with the years. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, for the sake of those who rely on its light, yet I confess, I have sometimes longed for the warmth of your presence.

I write to you now not out of idle sentiment, but because the tempest that approaches this evening is of such ferocity that I fear it may claim me. The winds howl like the wails of the damned, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very bones of this old frame. I have no doubt that I shall not survive the night, and I would be most sorrowed if my absence should be a source of pain to you. Yet, I write not to beg for your forgiveness, but to offer you this final testament of my thoughts and feelings.

I have always believed in the strength of our bond, though I fear that time and distance have eroded the very thing that once bound us. I have no wish to burden

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to thee with a trembling hand, for the tempest doth gather upon the horizon, and the wind doth howl like the wails of the damned. I have not spoken to thee for five long years, and now, as the stars doth set and the moon doth wax, I find myself in the throes of a most terrible storm, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Thou art my only child, and I have been a fool in not keeping thee in my thoughts. I have been a lighthouse-keeper, and I have kept the light burning for the ships that pass, but I have not kept the light of my love alight in my heart. I have been a solitary man, and thou art the only thing that doth warm me in this cold world.

**To My Dearest Daughter,**

My dearest child,  
I’ve not spoken to thee in five years,  
Yet here I write, for the last time,  
With ink upon my heart, and storm upon my soul.  

Thou art thy mother,  
Though I’ve not been near thee,  
Nor seen thy face,  
Yet I’ve not forgotten thee.  

The sea is wild,  
The sky is black,  
The winds are howling,  
The waves are wrathful.  

I’ve not seen the dawn,  
Nor heard the bell,  
Nor felt the light,  
Yet I’ve not lost hope.  

If I do not return,  
Thou art not alone,  
For thou art mine,  
And I art thy father.  

The storm shall pass,  
The fire shall burn,  
The waves shall break,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not think it will. These hands are trembling, not from the cold, but from the weight of words I have long kept penned. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and I confess, I have not written to you, nor have you written to me. The silence between us has grown like the sea—vast, unyielding, and at times, unfathomable.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to speak, not only to you, but to the ghost of our former bond.

You were but a spirited child, always questioning the world, ever curious, ever bold. I recall your laughter echoing through the halls of our home, your hands eager to turn the pages of books, your eyes alight with wonder. And yet, as the years have passed, I have watched you grow into a woman of your own making, and I have not been the father you once knew.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to thee with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since our last farewell, and the world has turned upon me like the tides upon the shore—ever-changing, ever-uncertain. I have kept the light burning, as I have sworn to the Crown, though my soul has often yearned for the warmth of thy voice and the touch of thy hand.

The night is now upon me, and the wind doth howl like a ghost upon the cliffs. The storm cometh, and the sea doth roar with a voice that maketh my bones to tremble. I know not whether I shall see the dawn, nor whether I shall see thee again. Yet even in this hour of peril, I think upon thee, and my heart doth ache with the thought that thou mightst be in some far-off land, or perhaps in the arms of some other, and that I mightst be left in the dark, with no light to guide me.

I have kept the lamp alight, as I have sworn, though my soul doth weep for the days we have lost. I have watched for thee in the hours of night, and the stars doth shine upon the sea as though they might guide thee.
